speaker: doctor
text: Good morning, Mr. Thompson. Based on your recent lab work and clinical evaluation, I’m diagnosing you with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, effective as of August 1st.

speaker: patient
text: Thank you, Doctor. I’ve been feeling more tired than usual, and I’ve noticed some increased thirst lately.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m also prescribing Atorvastatin 40 milligrams to be taken once daily at bedtime to help manage your cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk.

speaker: patient
text: Got it—I’ll start that tonight. Should I take it with food?

speaker: doctor
text: It can be taken with or without food. By the way, your diastolic blood pressure today was 97 mmHg—slightly elevated.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, really? I’ve been trying to cut back on salt, but maybe I need to monitor it more closely.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—and I noticed your heart rate was elevated at 112 beats per minute just now. Were you feeling anxious or short of breath?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s correct—your heart rate was 112 bpm during the initial assessment.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure reading at that time was 101 mmHg—also on the lower side, especially paired with the elevated heart rate.

speaker: patient
text: Hmm, I did rush in a bit—I walked up two flights of stairs before the appointment. Could that explain the high heart rate?

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, when I rechecked your diastolic pressure a few minutes later, it had risen to 110 mmHg—so it’s not just a transient spike.

speaker: doctor
text: And your systolic dropped to 90 mmHg on that second reading—quite a shift from the earlier 101. That pattern suggests possible orthostatic or autonomic influence, especially with diabetes.

speaker: doctor
text: Your repeat heart rate settled at 82 bpm—much more stable and within normal range—so the initial 112 may have reflected exertion or stress.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense. I’ve also been having occasional dizziness when standing up quickly—could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Very likely. Given your diabetes, blood pressure variability, and symptoms, we should evaluate for autonomic neuropathy and consider antihypertensive therapy that also protects the kidneys.

speaker: patient
text: Okay—what would you recommend?

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll hold off on adjusting the Atorvastatin for now, but I’d like to add a medication to help stabilize your blood pressure and provide renal protection.

speaker: patient
text: Is it something different from what I’m already taking?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—I’m going to switch you to Lisinopril 10 mg once daily in the morning. It’s an ACE inhibitor that helps control blood pressure and reduces diabetic kidney disease risk.

speaker: patient
text: Just to clarify—when I felt that racing heartbeat earlier, that was the same 112 bpm you noted at the start, right?
